1.synthesis: Paul sets Titus's positive task against the false teachers: speak the things that accord with sound doctrine (v.1). Then follows a catechetical program for every demographic in the congregation. Older men are to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, and in steadfastness (v.2). Older women are to be reverent in behavior, not slanderers or given to much wine — teachers of what is good, so that they may train the young women to love their husbands and children, to be self-controlled, pure, working at home, kind, and submissive to their husbands (vv.3–5). Young men are to be self-controlled in everything (v.6). Titus himself is to show himself in all things a model of good works — with integrity and dignity and sound speech that cannot be condemned (vv.7–8). Servants are to be submissive, well-pleasing, not stealing but showing good faith in all things, so that in everything they may adorn the doctrine of God our Savior (vv.9–10).
1.historical_context: Chrysostom on the older men's qualifications: there are some failings which age has that youth does not — slowness, timidity, forgetfulness, insensibility, irritability. The instruction to be vigilant addresses precisely these tendencies; the virtues of soundness in faith, love, and steadfastness are the specific antidotes to the characteristic failures of age. Gill on aged women as teachers: they are not to be placed in formal teaching office, but their role is to train young women privately in the domestic and relational virtues — what we might call mentoring. The servants' instruction closes the section with its most striking formulation: adorn the doctrine of God our Savior. The behavior of the lowest-status members of the household makes the gospel more or less attractive to the watching world.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured as a demographic catechism: older men (v.2), older women and their teaching role toward younger women (vv.3–5), young men (v.6), Titus himself as model (vv.7–8), servants (vv.9–10). The common thread is sound doctrine expressing itself in the virtues appropriate to each station. The inclusion of Titus as one of the groups (along with older men, older women, younger women, young men, and servants) makes the point that the minister must embody what he teaches — the model precedes the command. The adorning-the-doctrine phrase of v.10 functions as the section's telos: all the specific virtues are means to the end of making the gospel beautiful to outsiders.
1.theological_application: Gill on Titus showing himself a pattern of good works: it was not enough for the minister to deliver sound doctrine and exhort persons of different ages to the things that become it; he should through the whole of his conversation be a pattern. The minister's life is itself a form of teaching. Gill on sound speech that cannot be condemned: the public ministry should use the wholesome words of Christ and the doctrines of the gospel, not the enticing words of human wisdom; such speech should be plain, easy, and acceptable, that the adversary may have no cause to reproach it. The whole program of vv.1–10 is the practical outworking of the truth that knowledge of the truth accords with godliness (1:1): each station's virtues are the form godliness takes within that particular vocation.
1.christology: Every demographic's virtue list leads ultimately to adorning the doctrine of God our Savior — which is the doctrine of Christ, the Savior who will appear in v.13. The servant's faithful and honest service adorns this doctrine before a watching world; the older woman's wisdom in training younger women adorns it within the household; Titus's exemplary conduct adorns it in the church's public face. The logic is that Christ himself is the content of the doctrine being adorned, and lives lived in conformity to his character make him visible in the world that cannot yet see him. Sound doctrine produces good works because both have the same source — the grace of God that appeared in Christ.

11.pericope_label: The Grace of God Brings Salvation
11.synthesis: Paul grounds the catechetical program of vv.1–10 in a sweeping theological statement: for the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people, training us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age, waiting for our blessed hope — the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ (vv.11–13). He gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify for himself a people for his own possession who are zealous for good works (v.14). Titus is to declare these things, exhort and rebuke with all authority — letting no one disregard him (v.15).
11.historical_context: Chrysostom captures the theological weight of the section: having demanded from servants so great virtue — to adorn the doctrine of God our Savior in all things — he adds the just cause: for the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared. The ethical demands of vv.1–10 are not grounded in natural obligation or social convention but in the epiphanic appearance of divine grace. Gill on the grace of God appearing: this is a personification of grace — the grace of God in Christ Jesus became visible in history through his incarnation and ministry. The grace is both the redemptive act and the transformative power that enables the virtues commanded above. Gill on the blessed hope: Christ himself, the object and ground of hope, our hope (1 Timothy 1:1), who is our blessed hope, now awaited at his appearing, and the blessedness itself.
11.literary_structure: The theological section (vv.11–14) functions as the ground-clause for the entire demographic catechism of vv.1–10: for the grace has appeared. The structure is: appearing-in-the-past (incarnation, v.11) → its present effect (training in godliness, v.12) → waiting for the future (blessed hope, v.13) → the past cross-work that secured both present transformation and future hope (v.14) → the practical mandate that follows (Titus's authority to declare and enforce all this, v.15). The two appearances — of grace past and of glory future — form the eschatological brackets within which the Christian life of vv.12 is lived.
11.theological_application: Gill on who gave himself for us: not another, not merely his possessions, but himself — his whole human nature in union with his divine person, offered as the ransom. The purpose is double: to redeem from lawlessness and to purify a people for his own possession. The purification is not merely forensic (acquittal from guilt) but real (transformation of character), producing people who are zealous for good works — which loops back directly to the catechetical instructions of vv.1–10. The good works the demographic groups are called to are not the basis of their acceptance but the fruit of their redemption. Chrysostom presses the practical conclusion: from a consideration of what God has done, exhort to almost all virtue — the indicatives of grace motivate the imperatives of godliness.
11.christology: The appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ (v.13) is one of the New Testament's clearest applications of the title God to Jesus: the great God and Savior is a single person, Jesus Christ, whose appearing will be the revelation of glory. Gill reads great God as a title applicable to Christ as the eternal Son — in whom all the fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily — and Savior as specifying his redeeming function. The same person who gave himself for us to redeem and purify (v.14) is the one who will appear in glory (v.13); the one who appeared in grace (v.11) will appear in glory. Past grace, present transformation, and future appearing are all anchored in the single person of Jesus Christ.
